title: Creating Keyspace and Tables in Cassandra
description: Example CQL commands for setting up a Cassandra keyspace with tables for a sample application
explanation: Demonstrates keyspace creation with replication strategy, table creation with primary keys, and how to design for different query patterns using different partition keys

title: CRUD Operations in Cassandra
description: Example of Insert, Select, Update, and Delete operations in CQL
code:
-- INSERT (upsert - inserts or updates)
INSERT INTO ecommerce.products (product_id, name, category, price, stock, created_at)
VALUES (uuid(), 'Laptop', 'Electronics', 999.99, 50, now());

-- SELECT with consistency level
SELECT * FROM ecommerce.products
WHERE category = 'Electronics'
LIMIT 100;

-- UPDATE (similar to insert)
UPDATE ecommerce.products
SET stock = stock - 1
WHERE product_id = a0b1c2d3-e4f5-6789-abcd-ef0123456789;

-- DELETE specific column
DELETE stock FROM ecommerce.products
WHERE product_id = a0b1c2d3-e4f5-6789-abcd-ef0123456789;

-- DELETE entire row
DELETE FROM ecommerce.products
WHERE product_id = a0b1c2d3-e4f5-6789-abcd-ef0123456789;

-- Using TTL for auto-expiration
INSERT INTO ecommerce.verification_codes (code, user_id)
VALUES ('123456', 'user123') USING TTL 300; -- expires in 5 minutes
explanation: Shows Cassandra's upsert behavior (INSERT and UPDATE are similar), TTL usage for temporary data, and different delete operations
complexity: O(n) for reads where n is number of partitions scanned, O(1) for point writes

title: Cassandra Java Driver Connection Example
description: Basic Java code for connecting to Cassandra cluster and executing queries
explanation: Demonstrates connecting to Cassandra using DataStax Java Driver, executing queries, and handling results
complexity: Connection setup O(1), Query complexity depends on partition strategy
